metadata.title: Understanding Tax Filing Requirements
metadata.subtitle: Know When to File, What Forms You Need, and How to Avoid Costly Mistakes

content.title: Understanding Tax Filing Requirements
content.subtitle: Know When to File, What Forms You Need, and How to Avoid Costly Mistakes

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Ava earned $4,500 at her part-time job last year. Since she's below the $13,850 filing threshold, she assumed she didn't need to file taxes.

But her employer withheld $450 in federal income tax. By not filing, Ava is leaving $450 on the table—money that's rightfully hers!

Meanwhile, Ben earns $32,000 at his retail job plus $5,000 from freelance work, but didn't realize his freelance income triggers additional filing requirements.

How do you determine if you need to file taxes, and when might filing benefit you even if it's not required?

objectives.description: Determine who needs to file a tax return based on income, age, and filing status.
Identify common tax forms including W-2s, 1099s, and Form 1040.
Recognize important tax deadlines and consequences of missing them.
Evaluate when filing is beneficial even if not legally required.

terms.definition: Document from your employer reporting your annual wages and taxes withheld from your paychecks.
terms.example: Your employer sends this by January 31. It shows your total earnings and what was withheld for federal, state, and Social Security taxes.

terms.definition: Reports income from non-employment sources: freelance work (1099-NEC), interest (1099-INT), dividends (1099-DIV).
terms.example: If you did $600+ in freelance graphic design, you'll receive a 1099-NEC from that client.

terms.definition: The primary form individuals use to file their annual federal income tax return—where everything comes together.
terms.example: You report your W-2 and 1099 income on Form 1040, calculate what you owe, and determine your refund or balance due.

terms.definition: The minimum income level at which you're legally required to file a tax return. Varies by age and filing status.
terms.example: 2024: Single under 65 = $14,600. Below this, filing is optional—but often still beneficial!

content.headerTitle: 2024 Filing Thresholds: Are You Required to File?

leftPanel.title: Standard Income Thresholds
paragraphs: Earn LESS than these amounts? Filing is optional—but keep reading!

infoCard.title: You MUST File If:
items: You received advance premium tax credits
You had Health Savings Account distributions

content.headerTitle: When Filing Is Optional But SMART

leftPanel.title: File to Get Money Back!
paragraphs: Even if you're below the threshold, you should file if:
Taxes were withheld: Like Ava—her employer withheld $450. She won't get it back unless she files!
These credits can give you money even if you owe $0 in taxes.

highlightBox.text: The average EITC refund is over $2,500. Don't leave free money on the table!

leftPanel.title: The $400 Rule
paragraphs: If you earn $400 or more from self-employment, you MUST file—regardless of your total income.

highlightBox.text: Ben's $5,000 freelance income means he MUST file—even if his total was below the threshold!

infoCard.title: Extra Forms for Self-Employment:
items: Quarterly estimated payments may be required

layoutData.title: Mark These Dates on Your Calendar
paragraphs: Missing deadlines can cost you money in penalties and interest:
bulletPoints: January 31: Employers must send W-2s; clients send 1099s
April 15: Tax filing deadline (or next business day if weekend/holiday)
April 15: Last day to contribute to prior-year IRA
October 15: Extended filing deadline (if you requested extension)
Quarterly: Estimated tax payments due (April 15, June 15, Sept 15, Jan 15)

keyPoint.text: 📅 An extension gives you more TIME to file, but NOT more time to pay. Estimate and pay by April 15 to avoid penalties!

content.headerTitle: Penalties for Late Filing and Payment

leftPanel.title: What Happens If You're Late
paragraphs: 5% of unpaid taxes per month, up to 25%
0.5% of unpaid taxes per month, up to 25%
The failure-to-file penalty is 10x worse than failure-to-pay!

highlightBox.text: If you can't pay, FILE ANYWAY. You can set up a payment plan, but the file penalty is brutal.

content.headerTitle: Filing Options: Choose What Works for You

layoutData.title: How to Actually File Your Taxes
paragraphs: You have several options depending on your situation and comfort level:
bulletPoints: IRS Free File: Free software for income under $79,000—uses major tax software brands
IRS Direct File: New option for simple returns—file directly with IRS (limited states)
Tax Software: TurboTax, H&R Block, TaxAct—$0-$100+ depending on complexity
VITA Sites: Free in-person help for income under $64,000 (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance)
Tax Professionals: CPAs, Enrolled Agents—best for complex situations

keyPoint.text: 🎓 Students and low-income filers: VITA and Free File are excellent FREE options. Don't pay for simple returns!

scenario.name: Ava's Filing Decision
paragraphs: Ava (17) earned $4,500 at her part-time barista job. Her employer withheld $450 in federal taxes. She's under the $14,600 filing threshold, so she's not REQUIRED to file.
But if she doesn't file, she loses that $450 forever. It takes 20 minutes to file a simple return and get her money back.

scenario.name: Ben's Complex Situation
paragraphs: Ben (24) has two income sources: $32,000 from his retail job (W-2) and $5,000 from freelance graphic design (1099-NEC).
Even though his W-2 job handles withholding, his $5,000 freelance income exceeds the $400 self-employment threshold—he MUST file and will owe self-employment tax.

content.question:
Chris owes $2,000 in taxes but files 3 months late without requesting an extension.

Calculate Chris's penalties:
• Failure-to-file: 5% × 3 months × $2,000 = ?
• Failure-to-pay: 0.5% × 3 months × $2,000 = ?

What should Chris have done differently?

Based on your current situation—part-time job, no income yet, gig work, or other sources:

What tax forms do you expect to receive?

Do you think you'll be required to file, or would filing be beneficial even if optional?

questions.question: Which form reports your wages and taxes withheld from your employer?
Why should someone file a tax return even if their income is below the filing threshold?
A single person earned $12,000 in wages and $600 from freelance work. Are they required to file? Why?
What is the standard tax filing deadline, and what should you do if you can't file by that date?

takeaways.title: Know Your Threshold
takeaways.description: Filing requirements depend on income, age, and status—but self-employment over $400 always requires filing.

takeaways.title: File for Refunds
takeaways.description: Even if not required, file to recover withheld taxes and claim refundable credits like EITC.

takeaways.title: Know Your Forms
takeaways.description: W-2 = employment, 1099 = other income, Form 1040 = your tax return. Collect them all before filing.

takeaways.title: Meet Deadlines
takeaways.description: April 15 is key. File even if you can't pay—the failure-to-file penalty is 10x worse than failure-to-pay.

main.title: Tax Filing Decision Maker
main.description: You understand the rules—but can you apply them to real situations? Tomorrow you'll work through multiple scenarios to determine who must file, who should file, and calculate penalties for those who don't.

steps: Analyze 5 different taxpayer situations and determine filing requirements
Match income sources to the correct tax forms (W-2, 1099-NEC, 1099-INT)
Calculate penalties for late filing and late payment scenarios
Identify situations where optional filing is financially smart
Choose the best filing method for different taxpayer profiles

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
